Q1: Honestly, my study routine was a mess this semester. I'd plan to study for hours, but then I'd just sit there feeling overwhelmed and not actually getting anything done. I tried to dedicate around 32 hours a week, but a lot of that time was spent staring at materials or getting distracted because my head wasn't in the right place. I'm a hands-on learner, so sitting and reading online content felt like torture most of the time.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then panic when something was due and try to cram everything in at once. The anxiety would build up so much that I'd avoid logging in, and then the guilt would make it even harder to start. It was this terrible cycle where deadlines controlled everything, but even then I couldn't always pull it together.
Q3: Usually panic. I'd get an email notification about a deadline or realize I hadn't checked in for a while and the guilt would force me to log in. Sometimes a classmate would mention something in the discussion board and I'd feel like I was missing out. It was rarely a proactive thing—more like damage control when I realized how behind I was getting.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every morning with my coffee. But then life got overwhelming and I started associating the LMS with stress and failure. After that, logging in felt more like a punishment than a routine. The platform itself became something I dreaded.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful when I could focus on them—I watched a lot of them because I could pause and rewind when my brain wasn't cooperating. Discussion boards were decent too because seeing other students' perspectives helped things click sometimes. But honestly, nothing felt truly useful because I was always playing catch-up and never absorbing things properly.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just felt like walls of text that I couldn't process in my state of mind. The optional quizzes I skipped entirely because they felt like extra pressure I couldn't handle. I told myself I'd get to them later, but later never came because I was drowning in the required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it was something I could do with my hands or actively engage with. Practice problems where I could work through something step by step were better than just reading. But with my anxiety so high, even worthwhile activities felt pointless because I doubted I'd pass anyway.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're barely keeping your head above water with required work, optional stuff just adds to the pile of things you're failing at. Required activities had this weight of obligation that sometimes pushed me to do them, but optional ones were easy to dismiss when I was already overwhelmed.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a ton of materials to my laptop thinking I'd study offline without the stress of being logged in. I had textbooks and notes spread all over my desk. I'd highlight things, try to work through problems on paper, watch YouTube videos explaining concepts. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the LMS because being on there made me anxious.
Q10: There were nights where I'd spend hours with my notes and downloaded PDFs, trying to understand concepts, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd work through practice problems in a notebook, discuss things with friends over text, or watch external tutorials. The LMS only saw me when I was submitting something or watching their specific videos.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF, watch or read it once online, then keep coming back to it on my laptop for days. The LMS would show one view, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times offline. It was easier to study without the constant reminder of everything else I was behind on.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart. I was already struggling with anxiety, and then some personal stuff hit—family issues, money stress, the usual. I stopped attending classes almost entirely, which made it harder to understand the online materials. The sadness just settled in and made everything feel pointless. Getting out of bed became the achievement, not studying.
Q13: Absolutely. The workload felt impossible when combined with everything else in my life. Confusion about concepts made me avoid the materials because struggling with them made me feel stupid. Fatigue from sleepless anxious nights meant I couldn't concentrate even when I tried. My part-time job and extracurriculars ate into time I should have spent studying.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, maybe even watching a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd finish a reading and realize I hadn't absorbed a single word. It was like going through the motions to feel less guilty, but I wasn't actually learning anything. Just existing in the space without being present.
Q15: I postponed exams and major assignments constantly, even knowing they were killing my grade. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like if I didn't try, I couldn't fail. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace first, but that headspace never came. Avoidance became my coping mechanism, even though it made everything worse.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that got me to do anything, but they also paralyzed me. I'd watch a deadline approach with this growing dread, unable to start until the last possible moment. Then I'd rush through something subpar or miss it entirely. The LMS became this countdown clock of my failures rather than a tool for learning.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. When I got decent feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly, like maybe I wasn't completely hopeless. But negative feedback or poor grades just confirmed what I already believed—that I was failing and probably couldn't recover. After a while, I stopped checking feedback because I couldn't handle more bad news.
Q18: The structure felt rigid in ways that didn't work for me. Having 16 online courses meant constant deadlines from multiple directions, and I couldn't keep track. The pace assumed you were keeping up consistently, but once I fell behind, there was no way to catch up. It felt designed for students who weren't struggling, not for people like me.
Q19: The lack of hands-on activities made it harder—I learn by doing, not by reading and watching. The discussion requirements helped a little because at least I was interacting with people. But the heavy reliance on self-paced online content was brutal for someone with low motivation and high anxiety. I needed more structure and support, not more independence.
Q20: That was basically me. I'd log in, click around, watch videos, read discussions—trying to feel like I was doing something. But when it came to actually producing work, the anxiety would spike and I'd freeze. High activity can mean someone is struggling to engage meaningfully, not that they're learning. Sometimes presence is just avoidance of absence.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone studies mostly offline, knows the material from other sources, or is just efficient, their LMS activity wouldn't reflect their actual understanding. For me, it was the opposite—lots of desperate activity with poor results. But yeah, the LMS doesn't capture what happens in someone's head or their notebook.
Q22: Course logs can show when and how often you click on things, maybe patterns of last-minute cramming. But they miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state behind the clicks, all the offline work. They can't tell if someone watched a video while crying or if they actually understood it. They measure presence, not learning or struggle.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, feeling like you're part of the learning process rather than just surviving it. It's when you're curious, asking questions, wanting to understand rather than just pass. For me, engagement was rare this semester—I was mostly just trying to stay afloat and failing at that too.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was actually trying despite my results, or how much the anxiety and sadness affected every interaction with the course. It can't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials, the conversations with classmates outside the platform, or the CBT sessions I attended trying to get my head right. My engagement was a battle the data doesn't reflect.
